title: Judging Others, Judging Self
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-18-2025 -PM- Judging Others, Judging Self - Luke 6_36-45 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Our Universal Tendency to Excuse Ourselves and Judge Others
subpoints: We instinctively excuse our own faults and magnify the faults of others
Jesus knew our propensity to justify self and blame others, especially our enemies
As he concludes the section on loving enemies, he gives a corrective on judgment and mercy
The sermon's main point: to love as we ought, focus on showing mercy to others and judging our own sins

outline.title: To Love as We Ought: Show Mercy to Others (vv. 36-38)
subpoints: We can only show mercy to others when we personally know the merciful God—Ephesians 2:4-5
Showing mercy means not judging others with a condemning spirit presuming to know their motives
Showing mercy means forgiving those who have sinned against us—not optional but commanded
Showing mercy means being generous even toward those who have burdened us

outline.title: Forgiveness Is Commanded, Not Optional
subpoints: To forgive a sinner is to release them from the guilt and penalty of their sin
Christians who know God's great forgiveness of them must forgive others as well
We should not extend forgiveness verbally until the other person repents, but we must forgive in our hearts and be ready
An unforgiving spirit reveals a judgmental spirit opposed to God's mercy

outline.title: To Love as We Ought: Judge Our Own Sins (vv. 39-42)
subpoints: Judging our own sins is the prerequisite to helping others with their sins
Jesus' image of the log and the speck: we are prone to exaggerate others' faults and minimize our own
We cannot begin to love others as we should until we confront our own sins with God's Word
Hebrews 4:12 shows that the Word of God opens us to see what is truly in our hearts

outline.title: We Must Judge Ourselves Down to the Heart Level (vv. 43-45)
subpoints: A tree produces fruit after its nature—you will not get peaches from an apple tree
The fruit Jesus primarily refers to here is our words, which reveal what fills our hearts
If anger, bitter words, and blame characterize your speech, your heart is not right before God
The only way to have a good heart is to be born again by the power of God's Spirit

outline.title: Application: Where Does Our Treasure Come From?
subpoints: A good man has a good treasure in his heart—where does this come from?
It comes from meditating often on God's great mercy in Christ, filling the storehouse
How is mercy going to come out of us if mercy is not in us first?
Ask yourself: Am I marked more by a merciful spirit or by a critical, judgmental spirit?

summary:
Preaching from Luke 6:36-45, Pastor Lyro addresses Jesus' teaching on the relationship between mercy, judgment, and the condition of the heart. The sermon opens with a humorous illustration of how we all excuse our own faults and magnify the faults of others, then moves to the text's central command: be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.

The first section explores what showing mercy requires. To show mercy to others, we must first know the merciful God ourselves—Ephesians 2 reminds us that God, who is rich in mercy, acted toward us even when we were dead in trespasses and sins. In this context, showing mercy means three things: not judging others with a condemning spirit that presumes to know their motives, forgiving those who have sinned against us, and being generous toward those in need. Forgiveness is treated with particular care: it is not optional, it is not a feeling, and it may be withheld verbally until the other person repents, but it must always be present in the heart.

The sermon's second movement addresses self-examination. Jesus' image of the log and the speck illustrates our universal tendency to exaggerate others' sins while ignoring our own glaring failures. The prerequisite to helping someone else with their sin is dealing first with our own. Hebrews 4:12 shows that the Word of God is the instrument God uses to open us to see the true condition of our hearts.

The final section applies Jesus' teaching on trees and fruit. The fruit Jesus refers to is primarily our words—what comes out of our mouths reveals what is in our hearts. Angry, blaming, bitter words reveal a heart that is not right before God. The only solution is regeneration: a good heart is a born-again heart. And the content of that good heart—the treasure it stores up—comes from meditating on God's great mercy in Christ, so that mercy overflows toward others.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Luke 6:36-45

We're reading from the Gospel of Luke, chapter 6, starting at verse 36. Luke 6, verse 36, this is the Word of our Lord.

Therefore, be merciful, just as your Father also is merciful. Judge not, and you shall not be judged. Condemn not, and you shall not be condemned. Forgive, and you will be forgiven. Give, and it will be given to you: good measure, pressed down, shaken together, and running over will be put into your bosom. For with the same measure that you use, it will be measured back to you. And he spoke a parable to them: Can the blind lead the blind? Will they not both fall into the ditch? A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone who is perfectly trained will be like his teacher. And why do you look at the speck in your brother's eye, but do not perceive the plank in your own eye? Or how can you say to your brother, "Brother, let me remove the speck that is in your eye," when you yourself do not see the plank that is in your own eye? Hypocrite! First remove the plank from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck that is in your brother's eye. For a good tree does not bear bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit. For every tree is known by its own fruit. For men do not gather figs from thorns, nor do they gather grapes from a bramble bush. A good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings forth good. And an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart brings forth evil. For out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks.

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Lord, God, we pray that you open our eyes to see your glory in this text. Speak to us, for asking Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction

This week I read about a young man who was filling out the questionnaire for housing at college. He's moving away from home as a freshman and is going to be paired up with another young man. He's filling out the questionnaire and the mom, as moms do, is looking over his shoulder, trying to peek at the answers. By the question, "Do you make your bed regularly and do you consider yourself a neat person?" this boy checked both boxes yes. His mother read his answer and, knowing the truth was far from it, she asked why he did that. He replied, "Have you ever thought they might stick me with some slob?"

Now we are all prone to excuse our own faults and magnify the faults of others. You know how it goes. I am quiet. You are unassertive. He is a wimp. I am concerned. You're curious. He is nosy. I am thrifty. You are a bit tight. He's cheap. I drive with the flow of traffic. You go over the speed limit. He is reckless.

That's how we tend to think. We always excuse ourselves and judge others. And Jesus knew our common propensity to justify self and blame others. As he concludes the section of his sermon dealing with the requirement of loving even our enemies, he knew that we would try to dodge its demands by judging our enemies and excusing ourselves. He gives a strong corrective by showing how we should focus on showing mercy, not judgment, even towards those who have wronged us.

## The Structure of the Passage

He does that in verses 36 and 37 through 38. Then to help us apply it, he goes on to show that we must focus on judging our own sins, or we will be like blind men trying to lead blind men—verses 39 and 40. Only when we have judged our sins can we clearly help another person with their sins—verses 41 and 42. In fact, we must judge ourselves down to the heart level because only a good heart can produce good fruit—verses 43 through 45.

Jesus is teaching us this: to love as we ought, we should focus on showing mercy towards others and judging our own sins.

## Part 1: Show Mercy to Others (vv. 36-38)

To show mercy to others, we must personally know the merciful God ourselves. In verse 36, Jesus says: therefore, be merciful just as your Father also is merciful. In Ephesians chapter 2, verses 4 and 5, the Apostle Paul says, God, who is rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ.

If we don't know God, who is rich in mercy towards us, we are not going to be able to be merciful to others.

To show mercy to others in this context means three things: not to judge them, to forgive them, and to be generous towards them.

### Not to Judge

So we're to show mercy to others, which means not to judge them. In verse 37, Jesus says: judge not and you shall not be judged; condemn not and you shall not be condemned.

Now when Jesus commands us not to judge others, he does not mean that we should not evaluate others' actions, beliefs, or teachings. Neither does Jesus mean that as a church and as individual Christians we should overlook or tolerate serious sins or doctrinal error. To judge others in this context is to look down on them with a condemning spirit, presuming that we know their motives.

How many fights, if you're married—you've all fought, had some disagreement. How often in a marriage are we tempted to judge the other person based not on what they did or said, but on what we think their intentions were? But Jesus says to you: stop it. Don't judge others based on what you think their intent was. When we do that, we're showing a self-righteous spirit.

### To Forgive

To show mercy to others means to forgive them. In verse 37 again: forgive and you will be forgiven. To forgive a sinner is to release him from the guilt and penalty of his sin. Forgiveness is always costly because you're giving up the right to judge, the right to punish someone. You say, I am not going to do that. In Christ, I'm taking care of that.

Christians like us know that God has forgiven us much, so we must forgive others as well. And forgiving those who have sinned against us is not optional. It's not something you do when you feel like it. God says you forgive.

We should not extend forgiveness verbally to the one who wronged us until he repents, since God does the same toward us. But we must forgive the person in our hearts and be ready to forgive the instant that he repents, just as God is ready to forgive every sinner who turns to him in repentance.

I know this is hard. Just looking at my own heart—sometimes I don't want the fight to be over. So I don't forgive. I nitpick the repentance. I nitpick the words that were said. I say, no, this is not it. Not because there's no repentance, but because I don't want to let it go and grant to God the right to figure out what's going on.

An unforgiving spirit is that judgmental spirit which is opposed to God's mercy.

### To Be Generous

To show mercy to others means to be generous to them as well. In verse 38: give and it will be given to you, good measure, pressed down, shaken together, and running over will be put into your bosom. For with the same measure that you use, it will be measured back to you.

In its context, this verse means that even if we have been burdened by people that we have helped, we must continue to be generous to those in need, just as God generously showered his mercy on us. Jesus really meant it when he said it's more blessed to give than to receive. There's a blessing that goes beyond the material in the giving.

## Part 2: Judge Our Own Sins (vv. 39-45)

Secondly, to love as we ought, we must judge our own sins down to the heart level.

We need to judge our own sins or we'll be blind leading the blind. Can you imagine the metaphor? Jesus says it's going to be like the blind leading the blind—both wound up in the ditch.

### First Remove the Plank

In verses 41 and 42, the Lord says: and why do you look at the speck in your brother's eye, but do not perceive the plank in your own eye? Or how can you say to your brother, "Brother, let me remove the speck that is in your eye," when you yourself do not see the plank that is in your own eye? Hypocrite! First remove the plank from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck that is in your brother's eye.

Note that Jesus does not say that we should not help our brother with the speck in his eye. But rather, we should first take the log out of our own eye, so that we can see clearly to help him with his speck.

Jesus is humorously pointing out how prone we all are to focus on and exaggerate the faults of others, but to minimize or even ignore our own glaring faults. We are quick to blame others, but slow to blame ourselves.

If someone else is late for an appointment with me, I think, how inconsiderate—doesn't he know how busy I am? But if I am late for an appointment, I think, oh, he'll just have to realize that I'm a busy man, I couldn't help being late.

We won't begin to love others as we should, and we won't grow spiritually, until we begin to confront our own sins with God's Word. Hebrews 4:12: for the word of God is living and powerful and sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing even to the division of soul and spirit, and of joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart. And verse 13: there is no creature hidden from his sight, but all things are naked and open to the eyes of him to whom we must give an account.

How does God make everything naked and open? Through his Word. As we examine ourselves through his Word, we know who we are. And once God's Word helps us get the log out of our own eye, we will be much more compassionate in helping our brother with his speck.

### The Tree and Its Fruit

We must judge our own sins down to the heart level. In verses 43 through 45, the Lord discusses the tree and the tree's fruit.

Jesus' point is that a tree produces after its nature. You're not going to get peaches out of an apple tree. The fruit here primarily refers to our words, which reveal what fills our hearts. The Lord says: a good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings forth good, and the evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart brings forth evil. For out of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks.

What is inside comes out of our mouths. If you are often pouring out angry, bitter words that tear down others, that blame others for all your problems, then your heart is not right before God. And the only way you can get a good heart is to be born again by the power of God's Spirit.

## Application: Fill Your Heart with God's Mercy

Note that the good man has a good treasure or storehouse in his heart. Where does this come from? It comes from meditating often on God's great mercy in Christ, because the whole passage is about mercy. How is mercy going to come out of you if mercy is not in there? And how is mercy going to get in there? By meditating on the great mercy of God that God has had upon you.

So God's great mercy—fill your thoughts with it, and you will have a storehouse of mercy to pour out to others. We don't think about God enough. We're more worried about figuring out how to live, and those are good concerns—how to honor God as a husband, a wife, a parent, a friend. But we don't spend enough time thinking about who God is, meditating on his attributes. The more we fill our hearts with God and his mercy, the more we're going to be able to pour that out onto other people.

So ask yourselves these questions. Am I marked more by a merciful spirit or by a critical, judgmental spirit? What would the people around me say? Am I blaming God or others for my problems, or am I working at removing the log from my own eye? In your marriage, do you always think the other person is at fault for everything that's going on? Am I frequently judging my own life down to the thought level by God's Word? Is pleasing Christ the focus of my life?

To love others, especially those who have wronged us as Jesus commands, we must focus on showing mercy to others and on judging our own sins. And yet we are often good at doing the opposite—showing mercy to ourselves and judging others. May God give us the grace to be merciful people who are about judging ourselves and being merciful to others.

## Closing Prayer

Father, we thank you for your goodness to us. We thank you that you are a merciful God. Help us to learn from you to be merciful people, that we might display that to one another, for asking Jesus' name.
